name: Envoy AI Gateway
tagline: Route, authenticate, and rate-limit GenAI traffic on Kubernetes with Envoy Gateway
seoTitle: Envoy AI Gateway - LLM Gateway for Kubernetes
metaDescription: Envoy AI Gateway is an open-source extension to Envoy Gateway that routes app traffic to GenAI providers with auth, rate limiting, and routing on Kubernetes.
overview: Envoy AI Gateway is an open-source project that uses Envoy Gateway to handle request traffic from application clients to generative AI services. It sits between your apps and the LLM providers they call, so you manage that traffic with the same Envoy and Kubernetes tooling you already run for the rest of your platform.

It is built for platform and infrastructure teams who serve AI traffic from a Kubernetes cluster. It uses a two-tier pattern: a Tier One Gateway acts as a central entry point for authentication, top-level routing, and global rate limiting, while a Tier Two Gateway gives finer control over self-hosted model serving, including an endpoint picker for LLM inference optimization.

As an LLM gateway, it connects to many providers behind one entry point, including OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, Google Gemini, Vertex AI, AWS Bedrock, Anthropic, Mistral, Cohere, Groq, Together AI, and others, so clients can reach different backends without each app handling provider-specific setup.
features: Routes client traffic to many GenAI providers, including OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, Google Gemini, Vertex AI, AWS Bedrock, Anthropic, Mistral, Cohere, and Groq
Two-tier gateway pattern: a Tier One entry point for auth and global rate limiting, a Tier Two gateway for self-hosted model serving
Handles authentication, top-level routing, and global rate limiting at a central point
Endpoint picker support in the Tier Two gateway for LLM inference optimization
Builds on Envoy Gateway, so it runs as native Kubernetes resources managed with kubectl and Helm
Installed via Helm charts published as OCI artifacts, with CRDs and controller as separate releases

gettingStarted.intro: Envoy AI Gateway runs on Kubernetes as an extension to Envoy Gateway. Install the Helm charts, apply a basic route, then send an OpenAI-style chat completions request through the gateway.

steps.heading: Install the CRDs and gateway
steps.body: Install the Custom Resource Definitions, then the AI Gateway controller and resources, using the published OCI Helm charts. Wait for the controller to become available.
steps.code:
helm upgrade -i aieg-crd oci://docker.io/envoyproxy/ai-gateway-crds-helm \
  --version v0.0.0-latest \
  --namespace envoy-ai-gateway-system \
  --create-namespace

helm upgrade -i aieg oci://docker.io/envoyproxy/ai-gateway-helm \
  --version v0.0.0-latest \
  --namespace envoy-ai-gateway-system \
  --create-namespace

kubectl wait --timeout=2m -n envoy-ai-gateway-system deployment/ai-gateway-controller --for=condition=Available

steps.heading: Apply a basic route
steps.body: Apply the basic example manifest to create a working gateway and route configuration.

steps.heading: Port-forward the gateway
steps.body: Find the Envoy service created for the gateway and forward a local port to it.
steps.code:
export ENVOY_SERVICE=$(kubectl get svc -n envoy-gateway-system \
  --selector=gateway.envoyproxy.io/owning-gateway-namespace=default,gateway.envoyproxy.io/owning-gateway-name=envoy-ai-gateway-basic \
  -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
kubectl port-forward -n envoy-gateway-system svc/$ENVOY_SERVICE 8080:80

steps.heading: Send a chat completions request
steps.body: Call the OpenAI-style chat completions endpoint through the gateway to confirm traffic is routed.

useCases: Give applications one entry point that routes to multiple LLM providers such as OpenAI, Bedrock, and Gemini without per-app provider setup
Apply central authentication and global rate limiting to all GenAI traffic leaving a Kubernetes cluster
Serve self-hosted models with fine-grained access control and endpoint-picker routing through the Tier Two gateway
Standardize LLM access on existing Envoy and Kubernetes infrastructure instead of running a separate proxy stack
